name: Phi Ghost
description: The term 'Phi' in Thai encompasses a wide range of supernatural beings, often translated as ghosts or spirits. Phi can be benevolent protectors, mischievous tricksters, or vengeful entities depending on their origin, circumstances of death, and interactions with the living. They are deeply ingrained in Thai culture and folklore, influencing religious beliefs, daily practices, and artistic expressions. The specific characteristics and powers of a Phi vary significantly based on the type of ghost, such as Phi Pop (possessing spirits), Phi Krasue (floating head with entrails), Phi Tai Hong (ghosts of those who died violently), and many others. Belief in Phi serves as a moral compass, reinforcing social norms and cautioning against harmful actions. Offerings and rituals are often performed to appease or ward off malevolent Phi, while benevolent spirits may be venerated for protection and good fortune. The Phi represent a complex interplay between the natural and supernatural realms, shaping Thai society and individual perceptions of the world beyond the visible.
subtitle: Diverse spirits of Thai folklore
appearance: The appearance of a Phi Ghost varies greatly depending on the specific type of spirit. Some may appear as translucent humanoids, while others take on monstrous or grotesque forms. Certain Phi, like the Krasue, are depicted as a floating head with dangling internal organs. Others may manifest as shadows or whispers, making their presence felt rather than seen. The appearance often reflects the circumstances of their death or the nature of their earthly life.
habitat: Phi Ghosts are believed to inhabit a variety of locations, often those associated with death, misfortune, or liminal spaces. These include cemeteries, abandoned buildings, forests, crossroads, and even the homes of the living. They are particularly drawn to places where they experienced trauma or where their earthly possessions remain.
behavior: The behavior of Phi Ghosts is highly variable, ranging from benevolent guardianship to malevolent harassment. Some Phi are believed to protect their families or communities, while others seek revenge for perceived wrongs. Many Phi are mischievous tricksters, delighting in playing pranks on humans. Offerings and rituals are often performed to appease or ward off Phi, depending on their nature and intentions.

keyMyths.title: The Krasue
keyMyths.description: The Krasue is a fearsome female spirit with a floating head and trailing entrails. She preys on pregnant women and newborns, consuming their blood and vital organs, making her one of the most dreaded Phi in Thai folklore.

sources.author: Terwiel, B.J.
sources.work: Monks and Magic: Revisiting Thai Popular Religion
sources.description: Explores the role of spirits and magic in Thai society.
